1: A fulfillment: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The Passion Psalm that Jesus cited from the cross is both a historical lament (David in extremity) and a Christological prophecy (the Son's abandonment in the atonement). The cry 'why have you forsaken me' is not a failure of faith but a genuine experience of divine absence — the moment when Christ bore the full weight of human sin-under-judgment. The Psalm's movement (dereliction → trust → vindication → universal proclamation) is the death-and-resurrection narrative in miniature. Jesus did not die in despair; the Psalm he cited ends in the world's worship of YHWH through the one who suffered.
2: A direct connection: 'O my God, I cry by day, but you do not answer, and by night, but I find no rest.' The sustained divine silence that follows the cry of dereliction (v1, already present) is what Jesus experienced for the full duration of the crucifixion — from the sixth hour to the ninth (Matthew 27:45-46). The 'by day' and 'by night' suggest a duration that exceeds a moment: the forsakenness was not instantaneous but prolonged. The one who had never known divine silence (John 11:41-42: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me') entered it fully at Golgotha.
3: A revelation of God: 'yet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.' The suffering psalmist affirms the divine holiness even in the midst of forsakenness. The theological precision here is profound: it is precisely because YHWH is holy that the forsakenness occurs. 'He made him to be sin who knew no sin' (2 Corinthians 5:21) — the holy God cannot face sin without judgment; Christ bearing the world's sin faced the full force of that holiness. The holiness that caused the forsakenness is the same holiness that vindicates the resurrection.
4: A revelation of God: 'in you our fathers trusted; they trusted, and you delivered them.' The contrast that makes the psalm's suffering so acute: ancestors who trusted were delivered; the Son who trusts is not (yet) delivered. The patriarchs and Israel through the wilderness — all trusted and were saved. Jesus on the cross is the one for whom the pattern is temporarily reversed, precisely to secure the deliverance of all who come after. The deliverances of the fathers were types and pledges of the one deliverance that requires the non-deliverance of the Son.
5: A revelation of God: 'to you they cried and were saved; in you they trusted and were not put to shame.' The covenantal expectation: crying → salvation; trusting → not put to shame. Jesus fulfills the negative of this pattern temporarily at the cross (the trusting one is shamed publicly) in order to fulfill it absolutely in the resurrection. The shame of the cross becomes the ground for 'whoever believes in him will not be put to shame' (Romans 9:33; Psalms 10:11, quoting Isaiah 28:16).
6: A direct connection: 'but I am a worm and not a man, scorned by mankind and despised by the people.' The self-description of ultimate humiliation — 'a worm' (tolaat) — anticipates Philippians 2:7-8: Christ 'emptied himself, taking the form of a servant... humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' The scorned and despised one of Psalms 22:6 is identified in Isaiah 53:3 ('despised and rejected by men') as the Suffering Servant — and in the NT as Jesus crucified (Mark 15:31-32: the chief priests and scribes 'mocked him').
7: A direct connection: 'all who see me mock me; they make mouths at me; they wag their heads.' Matthew 27:39 cites this verse as fulfilled at the crucifixion: 'those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads and saying...' The physical gesture of head-wagging is not coincidental — the Gospel narrator recognizes it as the fulfillment of the specific gesture Psalms 22:7 describes. The entire crucifixion scene in Matthew is narrated through the lens of Ps 22.
8: A direct connection: 'He trusts in the LORD; let him deliver him; let him rescue him, for he delights in him!' Matthew 27:43 quotes this verse verbatim: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him. For he said, I am the Son of God.' The mockers at the cross unwittingly speak the script of Psalms 22:8, enacting the prophecy while intending only to taunt. The citation proves the fulfillment is not coincidental — the psalm was composed for this moment.
9: A direct connection: 'yet you are he who took me from the womb; you made me trust you at my mother's breasts.' The birth-trust established by YHWH from the beginning. Applied to Christ: the incarnation itself is the divine act of 'taking from the womb' — Luke 2:7, 11-12: 'she gave birth to her firstborn son... today in the city of David a Savior has been born to you, who is Christ the Lord.' The infant Jesus is the one YHWH brought from the womb for the specific mission of Ps 22.
10: A direct connection: 'on you was I cast from my birth, and from my mother's womb you have been my God.' The divine claim on Christ from his very entrance into human life. Luke 1:35: 'the Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy — the Son of God.' The total God-belonging of Psalms 22:10 is expressed in the annunciation: the one born is God's Son, cast upon God from the womb by divine design.
11: A shadow: 'be not far from me, for trouble is near, and there is none to help.' The petition for divine nearness in the moment of absolute isolation. At Gethsemane: 'Could you not watch with me one hour?' (Matthew 26:40) — the disciples failed to stay near. At the cross: 'they all left him and fled' (Matthew 26:56). The aloneness of Psalms 22:11 — no human help — was the condition of the crucifixion. The 'none to help' is precisely the situation that required Christ to be the only helper: he who had no human help becomes the helper of all.
12: A shadow: 'many bulls encompass me; strong bulls of Bashan surround me.' Bashan — the fertile highland region whose bulls were the largest and most powerful in the ancient world (Amos 4:1: 'cows of Bashan'). The powerful enemies surrounding the psalmist become, in Christ's passion, the combined forces of religious, political, and spiritual opposition — the Sanhedrin, the Roman soldiers, and the demonic powers all converging on Golgotha.
13: A shadow: 'they open wide their mouths at me, like a ravening and roaring lion.' The lion metaphor for the enemies who seek to devour — Peter uses this image for the devil himself (1 Peter 5:8: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour'). At the cross, the 'roaring lion' was unleashed against the Son of God. The resurrection is Christ's defeat of the lion: Hebrews 2:14 ('through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil').
14: A direct connection: 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint; my heart is like wax; it is melted within my breast.' The physical description of crucifixion: dehydration ('poured out like water'), joint dislocation from the position on the cross, and cardiac trauma ('heart like wax, melted'). Modern medical analysis of crucifixion confirms the cardiac rupture that John 19:34 may describe: 'one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once there came out blood and water' — the water from the pericardial sac consistent with cardiac rupture from the Psalms 22:14 description.
15: A direct connection: 'my strength is dried up like a potsherd; and my tongue sticks to my jaws; and you lay me in the dust of death.' The extreme thirst ('my tongue sticks to my jaws') directly anticipates John 19:28: 'After this, Jesus, knowing that all was now finished, said (to fulfill the Scripture), I thirst.' The word 'to fulfill the Scripture' in John 19:28 most likely refers to Psalms 22:15 (or Psalms 69:21: 'for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink'). The 'dust of death' is the grave into which YHWH 'lays' him — the burial that precedes the resurrection.
16: A direct connection: 'for dogs encompass me; a company of evildoers encircles me; they have pierced my hands and my feet.' 'Pierced my hands and my feet' — the crucifixion's specific physical detail, written a millennium before crucifixion was invented as a form of execution. John 20:25,27: Thomas demands to see 'the mark of the nails' in Jesus's hands. John 19:37 quotes Zechariah 12:10 ('they will look on him whom they have pierced') but the primary fulfillment of 'pierced hands and feet' is Psalms 22:16. The early church read Psalms 22:16 as predictive specifically of the nailing to the cross.
17: A direct connection: 'I can count all my bones — they stare and gloat over me.' 'I can count all my bones' — the emaciation or exposure of the body on the cross, where every rib and joint is visible. The 'staring and gloating' of the crowd at the crucifixion: 'the people stood by, watching' (Luke 23:35) while the rulers scoffed. The public spectacle of the cross — designed by Rome for maximum humiliation and visibility — fulfills the 'stare and gloat' of Psalms 22:17.
18: A direct connection: 'they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' John 19:23-24 cites this verse explicitly as fulfilled: 'the soldiers divided his garments among the four of them. They also took his tunic, but it was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom, so they said to one another, Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it to see whose it shall be. This was to fulfill the Scripture which says, They divided my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' One of the most precisely documented fulfillments in all the Gospels.
19: A shadow: 'but you, O LORD, do not be far off! O you my help, come quickly to my aid!' The renewed petition from the depths — the turn from lament to petition. The resurrection is the answer to this cry: YHWH did not stay 'far off' permanently. The speed of the divine response ('come quickly') was three days — not instantaneous, but not forever. The confidence beneath the petition is that YHWH will act; the psalm does not end in forsakenness.
20: A shadow: 'deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of the dog!' The 'sword' and the 'dog' — the instruments of violent death. Christ's soul was delivered from the sword through the resurrection: the 'power of death' (Hebrews 2:14) over which the devil presided was broken when God raised him. The 'precious life' (yechidati, my only one) corresponds to the 'only-begotten Son' (monogenes, John 3:16) — the uniquely precious one whose life was laid down and restored.
21: A shadow: 'save me from the mouth of the lion! You have rescued me from the horns of the wild oxen!' The answer to the petition arrives — 'you have rescued me' shifts the psalm to confident retrospect. This is the pivot of Ps 22: from the depths of forsakenness to the assurance of rescue. Applied to Christ: the resurrection is the moment of 'you have rescued me.' The lion's mouth and the wild oxen's horns (the forces of death and evil unleashed at the cross) are defeated by the divine rescue of the third day.
22: A direct connection: 'I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.' Hebrews 2:12 quotes this verse explicitly as words spoken by the risen Christ: 'he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise.' The post-resurrection community is the congregation where the risen Christ praises the Father through his people. The 'congregation' of Psalms 22:22 becomes the church — those who hear the name proclaimed by the risen one.
23: A theme: 'you who fear the LORD, praise him! All you offspring of Jacob, glorify him, and stand in awe of him, all you offspring of Israel!' The summons to the covenant community following the rescue — the resurrection community called to praise. The 'offspring of Jacob' and 'offspring of Israel' who praise the rescued one become, in the NT's universal reading, all who belong to the true Israel — those grafted in through faith in Christ (Romans 11:17-24; Galatians 3:29: 'if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring').
24: A revelation of God: 'for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help.' The theological climax of Ps 22: YHWH did not ultimately despise the suffering or hide his face permanently. The resurrection is the proof that the 'hiding of the face' at Golgotha was temporary — the Father heard the cry, and the answer was the empty tomb. The one who cried 'why have you forsaken me?' was heard, and the resurrection was the answer.
25: A theme: 'from you comes my praise in the great assembly; my vows I will perform before those who fear him.' The thanksgiving vow performed in the assembly — the todah (thanksgiving sacrifice) following deliverance. Christ's resurrection appearances in the midst of his disciples are the beginning of the 'great assembly' praise. The Eucharist (the Christian todah) is the ongoing performance of the vow: 'do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19) — the risen one's thanksgiving meal continued in his body.
26: A theme: 'the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied; those who seek him shall praise the LORD! May your hearts live forever!' 'The afflicted shall eat and be satisfied' — the covenant meal after the affliction: anticipating the resurrection appearances where Jesus ate with his disciples (Luke 24:30-31,41-43; John 21:9-13). The eschatological banquet that the afflicted share after the rescue of Ps 22 is the gospel feast, and ultimately the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9).
27: A theme: 'all the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations shall worship before you.' The universal missionary vision arising from the individual's rescue: the nations turning to YHWH because of what happened to the anointed sufferer. Paul's entire missionary theology is grounded here: the crucified and risen Christ is the reason 'all the families of the nations' have reason to worship. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20) is the activation of this vision.
28: A revelation of God: 'for kingship belongs to the LORD, and he rules over the nations.' The universal kingship of YHWH — declared as the ground of the nations' turning (v27). In the NT, this kingship is exercised through the risen Christ: 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18). The nations turn to YHWH (v27) because YHWH rules through his Son — the kingship of Psalms 22:28 is the inheritance of Psalms 2:8.
29: A theme: 'all the prosperous of the earth eat and worship; before him shall bow all who go down to the dust, even the one who could not keep himself alive.' 'All who go down to the dust' — the dead worship YHWH. This is among the earliest OT hints of resurrection worship: even those who have died bow before YHWH. Philippians 2:10-11 ('at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth') makes this universal: the dead as well as the living bow before the risen Christ.
30: A theme: 'posterity shall serve him; it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation.' The intergenerational transmission of the rescue-story — 'it shall be told.' The church's proclamation of the gospel (evangelism) is precisely this: telling the coming generation what YHWH did for his anointed one. The Great Commission establishes the mechanism: 'teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you' (Matthew 28:20). The story of Ps 22 is the story that 'shall be told' to every generation.
31: A direct connection: 'they shall come and proclaim his righteousness to a people yet to be born, that he has done it.' 'That he has done it' — ki asah — corresponds to the great 'It is finished' (tetelestai, John 19:30): the Greek perfect tense of completed-with-ongoing-results. The proclamation of Psalms 22:31 ('he has done it!') is the gospel: the work of rescue is complete. The people 'yet to be born' who will hear this proclamation are the Gentile nations — those who come to faith in every generation since the first Easter.
